# Introduction

Financial markets have undergone a long transition from predominantly
human-mediated trading toward electronic and increasingly automated execution.
This transition has been especially pronounced in high-frequency settings, where
decisions are made on very short horizons and depend on information contained in
the limit order book rather than only in aggregated price bars.

This distinction is methodologically important. A large share of prior research
on algorithmic trading and reinforcement learning evaluates models on
lower-frequency data such as daily or intraday OHLCV bars. Such representations
are useful for many forecasting and portfolio tasks, but they compress away much
of the market microstructure information that governs high-frequency trading.
At short horizons, the local state of liquidity, queue dynamics, spread
variation, and the timing of order book updates may be as important as the
direction of recent price movement.

For this reason, high-frequency trading should not be treated as a simple
higher-speed version of bar-based trading. The state observed by the decision
maker is structurally different. Best bid and ask quotes, depth imbalance,
microprice deviations, spread regimes, order-flow-related signals, and
event-time information provide a more direct description of short-horizon market
conditions than coarse candlestick aggregates. A central premise of this thesis
is that reinforcement learning agents should be evaluated in a state space that
reflects these microstructure properties explicitly.

Recent progress in machine learning and reinforcement learning has increased the
practical relevance of adaptive decision-making systems in financial domains.
Trading is naturally sequential: each action affects future exposure, portfolio
state, and the opportunities available at subsequent time steps. This makes
reinforcement learning a plausible modelling framework, provided that the data
representation, reward specification, and evaluation procedure are aligned with
the economic structure of the trading problem.

This thesis investigates the use of reinforcement learning, with particular
focus on TD3, for high-frequency single-asset trading based on
order-book-derived information. The research is motivated not by a claim that
artificial intelligence can universally solve trading, but by a narrower and
testable question: under controlled experimental conditions, can a continuous
control agent using microstructure-aware features achieve economically
meaningful out-of-sample performance relative to selected benchmarks?

The problem is challenging for several reasons. Financial rewards are noisy and
non-stationary, transaction costs can erase apparent profitability, and
short-horizon strategies are sensitive to feature design, turnover, and market
frictions. In addition, strong empirical claims are difficult because favorable
in-sample results may reflect data leakage, overfitting, or unrealistic
execution assumptions rather than genuine decision quality.

Accordingly, the thesis emphasizes methodological discipline alongside model
development. It treats feature engineering, chronological evaluation,
reward-return separation, and robustness analysis as core parts of the research
problem rather than secondary implementation details. In this setting, the
question is not only whether an agent can learn, but whether the learning setup
is sufficiently well specified to support credible empirical conclusions.

At the same time, the work does not claim to reproduce the full operational
complexity of live high-frequency trading. The empirical framework is based on a
controlled simulation environment rather than a complete execution engine with
queue-priority modelling, latency competition, exact fill uncertainty, and full
market impact. The contribution of the thesis should therefore be understood as
methodological and empirical: it evaluates reinforcement learning agents in a
microstructure-aware setting while making the simplifying assumptions explicit.
